In the meantime the political situation had greatly changed: in both London and the regions conflict had arisen over the possession of local rule, many Puritans had been become radicalised in their religious and socio-political views, and new reli-gious groups had emerged. Demands circulated among the public that went far beyond those that Parliament had put to the king at the start of the civil war. In certain places there had been social disturbances.
”翻译为中文

对于革命的结构分析来说，对立派系的确切社会组成可能不如以下事实重要：从1642年初夏开始，当王国权力分配因军事准备和从8月份开始的实际武装冲突受到挑战时，国王仍然得到了相当大的支持。此外，当国王于8月22日在诺丁汉竖立他的旗帜时，这也是一个信号，表明他准备通过武装力量迫使议会屈服于他的意愿。无论如何，内战前几年的军事斗争进程应该使任何急于从“前提条件”中推导革命（因而也包括其胜利）的历史必然性的人深思熟虑。

从长远来看，议会比国王拥有更多的资金，很可能总体上也拥有更坚定的支持。但在第一年的十六场主要战役中，保王党人，很快被称为骑兵，赢得了七场，不同的领导层本可以比保王党军官更有效地利用这些胜利进行军事利用。在战争的最初几年，议会能够获得进一步的物质资源并非确定的结论，这场战争绝对不是预先就为议会事业（因此也为“历史进步”，不论如何定义）赢得的。

只有因为议会一方的领导者能够与苏格兰人达成军事和教会政策联盟，并且因为他们最终也准备进行比战争开始时绝大多数人打算的更深远的政治改革，胜利才成为可能。

这些创新使得之前仅仅是一个咨询机构的议会变成了政府，很大程度上通过委员会进行自我组织。它们还影响到了税收形式。然而，最重要的是，它们涉及到武装力量的组织。在内战过程中，这一力量至少在初步上是国家化的。然而，这意味着武装力量（及其招募和供给的民事形式）不能自动融入到以前由贵族主要家族领导的地方性普遍贵族统治结构中。因此，革命的初始意图与战争彻底相反的政治发展的可能性的前提条件是这场战争的产物，更确切地说，是内战并不是预先决定为拥有更多物质资源者胜利的事实的产物。

起初，在战争的前两年，双方尽可能保持了传统的军事组织结构。作为其军队的最高指挥官，国王利用了新成立的战争委员会，以及可以由地区性“征召令”委员会指导和监督的地方行政人员。只要可能，地方法院或集会应参与这些委员会的措施。直到第二次内战结束，这种组织形式或多或少负责了国王武装力量的部署。起初，这是一个相当集中的结构，因为有一支在国王指挥下的单一军队，尽管它受到地方条件的强烈影响（地方军队指挥官与高级指挥部的关系仍然不清楚），招募和供给也是如此。保王党军队的资金既来自地方负担，也来自伦敦某些公司成员（如果不再是整个城市）的贷款，尤其是外国信贷。

与国王的武装力量相比，议会的武装力量最初要不那么集中，且具有双重军事结构：在地方建立和供应的民兵仍然处于郡中尉及其副手的地方指挥之下，对于这些人的选择，议会仍然坚持1642年任命贵族担任郡中尉和任命主要绅士家族成员为其副手的传统做法。议会本身承担了最高指挥权。但议会只直接控制着约1万人的军队。加上对政治中立的广泛渴望，以及因此拒绝在其原籍县外部署地方民兵，这一明显的组织分歧意味着议会的军事力量受到了重大限制。通过联合几个县的民兵来克服盛行的军事地方主义的尝试只有一个取得了真正成功：东部联盟。

为了筹集军队资金，议会最初主要依赖贷款。商人被“邀请”继续缴纳以往受到强烈反对的吨税和磅税，而各郡被要求捐赠金钱或贵金属。然而，各郡试图将钱款留在伦敦以装备其提供的武装人员，而不是用来支持议会军队。到了1643年春，议会发现自己被迫引入了一种新的融资系统。任何收入高于日工人的人都必须每周缴纳费用，富有的地主被迫提供贷款，而保皇党人的土地被没收并出售。这种税收是革命性的，因为它是在未经国王同意的情况下征收的。这种税收高于之前实行的税收形式，并且意味着对郡县传统统治形式的攻击。县级委员会成立以执行此税收，这些委员会的成员不一定是之前被视为在当地值得担任职务的人。

议会组织的武装力量既不是人民军队，也确实不是那些在讲道和请愿书中要求改革王国的敬畏上帝的人的军队。相反，它是一个特定贵族集团以大体传统的方式建立的军事组织，用于实现他们的政治目标。因此，许多士兵被强迫征召——就像以前的情况一样——他们中的许多人同样试图逃跑。剩下的人经常表现得如此缺乏纪律，以至于即使在对议会持友好态度的地区，人们也有充分的理由害怕他们。

1645年对议会武装力量进行的改革并没有立即从根本上改变这种情况，正如马克·基斯兰斯基在一项开创性工作中所展示的那样。新模范军最初既不是政治上的激进，甚至也不是政治上的统一。因此，它不是议会中激进派的武装力量。新模范军也没有立即废除先前对武装力量的普遍贵族统治的做法。

到1644年底，军事局势给了许多虔诚的人一个机会，考虑他们如何能让议会事业更加讨上帝的喜欢，以及他们可以采取哪些实际措施充分参与上帝的恩典。就普遍政治结构而言，这种政治上活跃的虔诚统治者的自我批评很快聚焦于个人统治的那些方面，这些方面过去使得从官职中获得个人荣誉和利益成为可能和惯例，而促进“公共福祉”最多只能排在第二位。1644年11月，成立了一个委员会来调查官职收入的哪一部分仍应普遍保留，到了12月，下议院通过了所谓的“自我否定令”，要求两院的成员自愿放弃公职，包括文职和军职。正如克伦威尔在议会中所说，这样一来，我们的敌人就不能说两院的任何成员对战争的迅速结束没有兴趣，因为这给了他们机会利用自己的职务谋取个人进步。

当伦敦民兵队长托马斯·朱克森声称，议员们第一次表现得像英格兰的平民，代表人民的福祉时，他表达了这一决定将导致统治结构非常大程度去人格化的观点。然而，贵族们不愿意为赢得上帝的恩典和不给敌人提供论据而走得那么远。这将使他们放弃个人军事力量的最后残余。如果这种力量不再构成法定固定的占有，大贵族们仍然维持着对指挥职位的传统确立权利。上议院拒绝了提议的立法，反而提议两院的成员只需放弃其文职。到了1645年4月，这一立场——标志着两院间关系恶化的开始，议会事业支持者之间出现了贵族结构化的冲突——已无法继续。伴随着军事力量的集中——虽不完全，但相对于先前结构明显加强——议会在预先免职了先前的指挥官后，任命了新的高级指挥官。人事选拔再次引发了两院之间的某些争议，据基斯兰斯基所述，上议院对下议院提案的修正部分是出于保护利益，部分是出于军事考虑，还有一部分——无法精确衡量——是出于宗教取向。军事策略现在将由两个王国的委员会确定，刚刚被解职（或退休）的将军们，埃塞克斯伯爵和曼彻斯特伯爵，仍是委员会成员。然而，最重要的是，新模范军仍然是议会的工具。其通过改革和两位新指挥官的政策增强的军事效能最终在第一次内战中取得了胜利。

1646年，国王在投靠苏格兰人后签署了投降声明。议会在与国王交战后，现在面临着要解决和平条款的局面。与此同时，政治局势已经发生了很大变化：在伦敦和地区出现了对地方统治的争夺冲突，许多清教徒在宗教和社会政治观点上变得激进，新的宗教团体出现了。公众中流传的要求远远超出了内战开始时议会对国王提出的要求。某些地方出现了社会骚乱。

请将“The revolutionary attack on generalised estate rule
The Revolution of 1648-9 grew out of the attempt of a considerable section of the lords to persuade the king to make peace, so that by abandoning earlier demands they could jointly defend their rule, now endangered by politically radicalised forces, social climbers, and substantial social unrest. To prevent this and create instead a more just world was the goal of the revolutionaries, who - in varying and somewhat contrary demands - aimed at 
the introduction of a representative constitution with general elections, the abolition of the monarchy, reform of the judicial system, the establishment of a better order in the church (if not the actual preparation of the realm of God on earth), and the abolition or attenuation of former social injustices. If this actually led to revolution, a precondition for this was the formation of a radical political and religious public, and especially the fact that large parts of both the military and administrative power and the press were de facto in the hands of 'radicals'. It was against what they saw as a dangerous situation that had arisen, that a parliamentary majority that had become or remained 'conservative' made their proposals to the king.
Important new features of the situation were changes in the army and the form of regional administration. For four years, soldiers had been billeted on the population, requisitioning provisions and demanding horses, and even in localities under parliamentary control there had been attacks on civilians, not to mention the costs of this plague, which the County Committees met by drastic measures. If we can trust the words of their opponents, these committees were made up of outsiders who failed to pay due respect to the old-established families of the local nobility. They even included a number of artisans - on the Isle of Wight for example a hawker, an apothecary, a baker and two farmers - as well as others who were in no way members of the ruling estates.231 On the whole, the committees were run by men with a certain level of wealth and education. But there was certainly a clear tendency for the former restrictive estate selection for office in the local administrations to be at least widened to a certain degree, if not completely abolished.
When Parliament decided to disband the army and send a part of it to Ire-land, to put down the Catholic rebellion there and prevent a possible alliance with the king, this corresponded with the demands of many petitions that had been received from London and the regions. When peace proposals were put to the king, providing for the immediate abolition of the County Committees once these were accepted, this followed the wishes of many lords who had until recently supported the parliamentary cause.232
The extent to which the political situation had changed is shown by the local defence forces in Somerset - ultimately under the control of the local sheriff - who tried to imprison marauding soldiers who crossed the county on their way to Ireland. In some districts, soldiers also laid siege to the County Committees, demanding payment of their outstanding wages.233
The army opposed demobilisation without the settlement of arrears of pay going back at least a year, and without arrangements that would guarantee the fulfilment of demands previously raised. Many members of the County Committees were radicalised when threatened with the loss of office.
Mark Kishlansky has ascribed the cause of political radicalisation in the first place to the decision taken by Parliament to disband the army, with no concern for the material interests of the soldiers, and secondly also to the unwillingness of soldiers to be sent to Ireland.
The Levellers above all maintained that it would be
an unlawful war, a cruel and bloody work to go destroy to the Irish Natives for their Consciences, and to drive them from their proper natural and native rights.234
Kishlansky's line of argument does not go far enough. He certainly shows that the politicisation of the army took place later, and the agitational success of the Levellers among the soldiers was less, than had long been supposed by historians; that material interest formed the basis of the (limited) political unity of the army and a precondition for its development into a factor of political power. But even those soldiers pressed into the army must have heard the political rhetoric that was developed to justify their bloody work. If a declara-tion of 1647 stated that they were not a
mere mercenary Army, hired to serve any Arbitrary power of a State: but called forth by several Declarations of Parliament, to the defence of our owne and the peoples just rights and liberties,235
this certainly formulates a political opinion that only became widespread as a result of the specific developments of that year. This does not mean, however, that it did not already have a basis in earlier experiences.
The organisation of resistance in the army began, moreover, in the cavalry, which, unlike the infantry, was almost entirely manned by volunteers, with a somewhat higher level of education than the compulsorily enlisted soldiers.
Early in 1647, the cavalry regiments commanded by Cromwell elected 'agents'. The Levellers, supporters of a radical political tendency that at the time of its greatest influence had a regular organisation with formalised rules and membership dues, drew up a petition to the Commons. This was signed by
Many thousands, earnestly desiring the Glory of God, the freedome of the Common-Wealth and the peace of all men.236
Even before this petition was officially submitted to Parliament, the majority of Members expressed their opposition to the demands it raised, refused further petitions and threatened to punish the objectors for disturbing the peace.237 People who had just begun to insist on their civil rights were thus refused access to the public sphere. March 1647 saw the start of an open campaign for public support. Parliament reacted not just with bans and the threat of punishment, but with the (difficult) procurement of a credit from London merchants for the purpose of paying arrears of at least six weeks, and above all the attempt to persuade the king to accept its demands. In the army, however, there was organisation of resistance - which Parliament branded as rebellion. In Oxford, too, a printing press was established for the purpose of making the demands of the soldiers known to the people.238 It was no longer Parliament they were addressing (the delegation Parliament sent to negotiate had to withdraw empty-handed), but rather a broad public that was becoming revolutionary.
In this public conflict with the established forms of rule in the kingdom, the agitators in the army, the Levellers who were especially active in London, and the many new (often chiliastic) religious groups that objected to any kind of political regulation of belief, all gathered increasing strength.
Parliament reacted with a stress on the traditional privilege that its debates should not be put under pressure, and thus refused the public any influence on its discussions. From the standpoint of the lords' traditional privileges, the 
demand (raised in particular by the Levellers) that the influence of the public should be institutionalised meant genuine rebellion - despite the fact that for centuries Parliament had acted on the basis of petitions, and in the first years of the Revolution certain members had themselves promoted the drafting of petitions. The question at issue, as Kishlansky has shown, was not whether there should be a complete end to petitions, but rather the privilege of Parlia-ment to select from the public sphere those aspects that coincided with the views of the majority in the House. Besides bans and sporadic offers to negoti-ate, the representatives of the previous ‘good order' had little to oppose to the increasingly revolutionary public both within and without the army. When in June the ‘glorious decision' of the army not to let itself be disbanded before the soldiers' demands were met was accepted in the regiments by acclamation, this meant that the practical possession of armed force would be turned to political account. The army now acted in fact as a power potential in its own right.
To implement this strategy an army council was created, by expanding the council of war of the senior officers that already existed by two junior officers and two private soldiers from each regiment.
When the parliamentary majority called the London militia to arms, this attempt to put the army back under the control of Parliament was halted by its advance on London.
In June, a junior officer - Joyce - and the soldiers under his command made the king a prisoner of the army, and the political demands formulated by its representatives now grew more far-reaching. In this first phase of politicisation of the army, political dominance lay with private soldiers, junior officers and a few radical higher officers. Generals Fairfax and Cromwell, together with the senior officers known as ‘grandees', now came to represent in their political milieu the demands of the rank and file. They also accepted the imprisonment of the king. In this way, they retained at least formal power of command over the de facto holders of armed force. By their struggle for the power to interpret the demands put forward by the army, and by use of the armed force at their disposal, they further reinforced and expanded their military command - and in the current situation, their political command as well. The struggle as to the contents of the army's demands was waged in the so- called Putney debates, the struggle over military command in the draconian suppression of the Ware mutiny. This arose because the army council had 
suppressed a resolution from the Levellers demanding universal suffrage (for every man except servants and beggars), and because it was also supposed that the 'grandees' had had a hand in the king's escape. With the suppression of this mutiny, the supremacy of the military command over the dynamic of public debate among the soldiers was decisively manifest. It was no accident that after this incident the influence of the Levellers in the army fell. This equally meant a decline in the possibility of a revolution that was genuinely determined by the people, or even a social revolution. A precondition for revolution is certainly a revolutionary public, but revolutions are decided not by argument, but by the effective threat or actual use of force. In 1647 an effective threat of force did indeed exist within the army, in both directions. In the later months of the year, therefore, a kind of political compromise was arrived at: for the sake of the ability of the army to act politically, the radical agitators accepted the command of the grandees, and these enforced military discipline by taking on some of the Levellers' demands - in particular the end of negotiations with the king.”翻译为中文

对普遍贵族统治的革命性攻击
1648-9年的革命源于诸多贵族劝说国王和平的尝试，通过放弃早期的要求，他们可以共同捍卫现在受到政治激进分子、社会攀爬者和大量社会动荡威胁的统治。预防此事并创建一个更加公正的世界是革命者的目标，他们提出多种不同且有些相反的要求——引入带有普选的代议制宪法，废除君主制，改革司法体系，建立更好的教会秩序（如果不是在地球上实际准备天国的话），以及废除或减轻以前的社会不公。如果这真的导致了革命，一个先决条件是形成了激进的政治和宗教公共领域，特别是大部分军事和行政权力以及媒体实际上掌握在“激进分子”手中的事实。正是面对他们认为已经出现的危险局势，已经变得或仍然保守的议会多数向国王提出了他们的提议。

形势的重要新特点是军队和地区行政形式的变化。四年来，士兵驻扎在民众中，征用物资和要求提供马匹，甚至在议会控制的地区也有对平民的袭击，更不用说这场灾难的成本，这是由县级委员会通过严厉措施来应对的。如果我们相信他们的对手所言，这些委员会由不了解当地老牌贵族家庭应得的尊重的外来者组成。他们甚至包括一些手工艺人——例如在怀特岛上有一个小贩、一个药剂师、一个面包师和两个农民——以及其他根本不是统治阶层成员的人。总体而言，这些委员会由一定程度的财富和教育水平的男性管理。但肯定有一个明显的趋势，即对地方行政中职位的传统贵族选拔至少在一定程度上被扩大，如果不是完全废除的话。

当议会决定解散军队并派遣其中一部分前往爱尔兰，以镇压那里的天主教叛乱并防止与国王可能的联盟时，这符合伦敦和地区收到的许多请愿书的要求。当向国王提出和平提议时，一旦这些提议被接受，立即废除县级委员会，这符合许多直到最近还支持议会事业的贵族的愿望。

政治局势的变化程度可从索美塞特郡的地方防卫力量体现出来——最终受当地警长控制——他们试图监禁穿越该郡前往爱尔兰的掠夺性士兵。在一些地区，士兵还围攻县级委员会，要求支付他们未付的薪水。

军队反对在未解决至少一年的欠薪和未做出保证以前提出的要求得到满足的情况下解散，并且许多县级委员会的成员在面临失去职位的威胁时变得激进。

马克·基斯兰斯基首先将政治激进化归因于议会解散军队的决定，而不顾士兵的物质利益，其次也归因于士兵不愿意被派往爱尔兰。

平等派首先坚持认为前往爱尔兰进行战斗将是
一场非法战争，一项残酷而血腥的行动，旨在为了他们的信仰而摧毁爱尔兰土著，并将他们从他们固有的自然和本土权利中驱逐。

基斯兰斯基的论证并没有走得够远。他确实表明，军队的政治化发生得较晚，平等派在士兵中的宣传成功也不像历史学家长久以来所认为的那样，物质利益构成了军队（有限的）政治统一的基础，也是其发展成为政治力量的先决条件。但即使是被强征入伍的士兵也必定听说了为证明他们血腥工作所做的政治说辞。如果一份1647年的声明声称他们不是
纯粹的雇佣军队，被雇佣来为国家的任意权力服务：而是被议会的几份声明召唤，来捍卫我们自己和人民的正当权利与自由，

这显然表达了一种政治观点，只是由于那一年的特定发展而变得广泛。然而，这并不意味着它之前没有基于早期经验。

此外，军队中的抵抗组织始于骑兵队，与步兵队不同，骑兵队几乎完全由志愿者组成，受教育水平略高于被强制征募的士兵。

1647年初，克伦威尔指挥的骑兵团选举了“代理人”。当时影响力最大的平等派，是支持激进政治倾向的团体，有定期组织、正式规则和会费，起草了一份向下议院的请愿书。这份请愿书上签名了
许多成千上万的人，他们急切地渴望上帝的荣耀，共和国的自由和所有人的和平。

即使这份请愿书还未正式提交给议会，大多数议员已经表示反对其中提出的要求，拒绝进一步的请愿，并威胁说要因扰乱和平而惩罚反对者。因此，刚刚开始坚持自己公民权利的人们被拒绝进入公共领域。1647年3月，公开争取公众支持的活动开始。议会不仅用禁令和惩罚威胁做出反应，还通过（费力）从伦敦商人那里获得信贷以支付至少六周的欠薪，最重要的是，试图说服国王接受其要求。然而，在军队中，抵抗得到了组织——议会将之标记为叛乱。在牛津，也为了让士兵的要求为人民所知而设立了印刷机。他们不再向议会发出呼吁（议会派来谈判的代表空手而归），而是向变革中的广泛公众发出呼吁。

在这场与王国既定统治形式的公开冲突中，军队中的激进分子、尤其是在伦敦活跃的平等派，以及反对任何形式的信仰政治调节的许多新兴（通常是千年王国主义的）宗教团体，都获得了越来越大的力量。

议会通过强调其辩论不应受到外部压力的传统特权来回应，并因此拒绝公众对其讨论的任何影响。从贵族的传统特权立场来看，平等派特别提出的要求（使公众影响得到制度化）意味着真正的叛乱——尽管几个世纪以来议会一直基于请愿行事，在革命的最初几年中某些成员还促进了请愿书的起草。争议的问题，正如基斯兰斯基所示，并非是否应该完全终止请愿，而是议会的特权是从公共领域中挑选出与下议院多数观点一致的方面。除了禁令和零星的谈判提议外，之前“良好秩序”的代表对日益革命化的内外公众几乎没有什么反对意见。当6月军队“荣耀地决定”不在士兵的要求得到满足前解散时，这一决定在团队中得到欢呼，这意味着实际拥有武装力量的人将政治利用这一力量。军队现在实际上作为一种自身的力量潜力而行动。

为实施这一策略，通过将已有的高级军官战争委员会扩大以包括每个团的两名初级军官和两名士兵而创建了一个军队委员会。

当议会多数派召集伦敦民兵时，这一试图使军队重新受到议会控制的尝试被军队向伦敦推进所阻止。

6月，一名初级军官——乔伊斯——和他指挥下的士兵将国王变成军队的囚犯，军队代表提出的政治要求变得更加深远。在军队政治化的第一阶段，政治主导权在于士兵、初级军官和少数激进的高级军官。法克斯将军和克伦威尔以及被称为“大人物”的高级军官，现在在其政治环境中代表士兵的要求。他们也接受了国王被监禁。通过这种方式，他们至少保留了对实际掌握武装力量者的正式指挥权。通过争夺对军队要求解释的权力，以及使用其手中的武装力量，他们进一步加强和扩大了自己的军事指挥权——在当前形势下，也是他们的政治指挥权。关于军队要求的内容的斗争在所谓的普特尼辩论中进行，关于军事指挥权的斗争在对沃尔反叛的严厉镇压中体现。这起因为军队委员会压制了平等派提出的要求普遍选举权（对每个人除了仆人和乞丐）的决议，也因为人们认为“大人物”在国王逃跑中起了作用。通过镇压这次反叛，军队指挥部对士兵公共辩论动态的控制权显著体现。在这一事件之后，平等派在军队中的影响力减弱。这同样意味着由人民真正决定的革命，甚至是社会革命的可能性下降。革命的一个先决条件当然是革命性公共领域，但革命的决定不是靠争论，而是靠实际使用武力或有效威胁武力。1647年，军队内部确实存在武力的有效威胁，两个方向都是如此。因此，在那一年的后几个月，一种政治妥协达成了：为了军队的政治行动能力，激进的激进分子接受了大人物的指挥，而这些大人物通过采纳一些平等派的要求——特别是结束与国王的谈判——来执行军事纪律。

请将“In this way, there arose a moderately radical political position of the army (though it contained neither a programme for specific regulation of the church, nor even - as was often supposed - the demand for complete freedom of con-science). In 1648 the so-called Second Civil War settled the rival claims for power by military means. Its history, as Winston Churchill pertinently wrote, was 'short and simple'. The king, the Lords, the Commons, members of the gentry and merchants, City and country, bishops and Presbyterians, the Scot-tish army, the population of Wales and the navy, all now fought against the New Model Army. And this army defeated their combined forces.239
To summarise these events would mean introducing many different ele-ments - anti-Puritan popular uprisings and resistance,240 petitions and mass demonstrations against the County Committees,241 the pressure of London merchants for a reconciliation with the king, the mutinies in the navy and the struggles of the Scottish army that took the field on the king's side, as well as the public justifications that the parliamentary forces distributed across the country.242 All this we must leave aside here. 
After the military victory over the royalist forces and all who had risen up against the army and the County Committees, in August 1648 Parliament began peace negotiations with the king. It demanded command over the mili-tia for a period of twenty years, the participation of Parliament in the selection of the king's advisers and a Presbyterian church order. The parliamentary majority - including many Members who could earlier have been counted among the Independents - were prepared to accept the few concessions that the king finally agreed, despite massive protest and a major written complaint from the army, as the basis for a reconciliation with the monarch. This already shows how much more was at stake now than the extent of monarchical power. For the same reasons that led to the more successful attempt of 1660, the parliamentary majority voted to restore the monarchy on 4 December 1648 - ‘eleven years too early'.243
This development was prevented two days later, when the army took civil power into its control. Parliament remained in existence, but politically it was ‘purged' of those members who supported reconciliation with the king. ‘Pride's purge', as the imprisonment of a number of MPs by Colonel Pride came to be called, made possible the condemnation and execution of the king. It was the last concession of the moderate forces in Parliament and the gran-dees to the radical popular movement within the army.
After the execution of the king in January 1649, England became a republic (although even then, many MPs would have preferred the appointment of a new king to the abolition of the monarchy). The House of Lords was abolished, followed in September 1650 by the penal code that since the Elizabethan religious settlement had enforced Sunday attendance in church. But this nearly completed the institutional results of the Revolution of 1648-9. There was no more mention of the Levellers' demands for a democratisation of estate rule. Instead of this, the radical leaders of the Levellers were imprisoned, and a mutiny by radical regiments in May 1649 was violently put down. The army thus became once more an instrument of politics.
The specific form of estate rule this instrument was now to defend, however, was not yet decided. The following eleven years until the formal restoration of the monarchy - for in practice, a far-reaching personal possession of centralised power was re-introduced already under the Protectorate - were 
therefore marked by conflicts over political supremacy, or more accurately, over the structure of power-holding within the ruling estates.
There continued to be demands for the abolition of estate privilege. But with the defeat of the 1649 mutinies, the possibility - however weak - of implement-ing social-revolutionary or even republican demands was finally removed.244 From now on, all those seeking far-reaching change were forced back again to arguments and forms of expression that were treated as 'rebellion' according to traditional practice. The religious radicals, and in particular champions of a 'fifth monarchy' (that of the Lord Jesus Christ, which was to redeem the previous four sinful periods of rule) could still have a certain influence. For, in the seventeenth century, the possible scope of religious arguments was still substantially greater than that of political and social-critical ones. But even hopes for the coming of the Kingdom of God on earth could not prevail in the long run against more immediate ruling interests. No more was this possible for those forms of communal possession of land that the 'true' Levellers (better known as the Diggers) began to put into practice in a number of small communes after 1649. In 1651 these experiments were forcibly ended.
The republican, social-revolutionary and chiliastic popular movements competed with one another in their aims, and did not provide any basis for continuing the Revolution. They did, however, present a provocation to the present holders of power, and, to this extent, they also influenced the conflicts over its distribution.245 Nonetheless, the political struggles during the years that were later described as the Interregnum were essentially an affair between 'gentlemen'. The political constellations in these changed: especially between the 'new forces' in the county administrations and the army commanders. The political dominance of the latter peaked in 1655 when 'majorgenerals' were introduced as local governors with both military and civilian responsibilities (similar to the French intendants). This maximal centralisation of political power - for the major-generals were executors of a central government that was controlled (if not completely dominated) by the military - meant a still more far-reaching attack on the generalised estate rule of the English nobles than had already been institutionalised in the County Committees. Obstruction and open resistance quickly undermined the basis 
of this centralised power. This could happen because the government was dependent on the support of the ruling estates for its foreign policy - based on religion and trade and prosecuted by armed force. The abolition of the major-generals in 1657 marked Oliver Cromwell's attempt at a new political alliance. The personal rule with which the Protectorate had invested him had its foundation in his possession of military power. For its practice, however - just like the personal power that the king had possessed - this depended on the collaboration of a large proportion of the members of the ruling estates. Cromwell did indeed manage to find among these a supporting group for his political strategy: the men historians nowadays refer to as the ‘new Crom- wellians'. The request made to Cromwell in 1657 that he should be crowned king illustrates the content of this; though Cromwell's refusal indicates how he was well aware that acceptance would lose him the support of the army and thus an indispensable basis for his rule.
The personal rule that the Lord Protector possessed was on the one hand institutionally reduced in comparison with that of the executed king, while on the other hand the presence of a standing army immensely expanded it. Exercise of this rule, however, demanded the collaboration of ‘gentlemen' - as otherwise neither the financing nor the authority of the new structures of office would be achievable. If the Protectorate government could maintain itself even though many of the leading noble families refused their collaboration or were excluded from it, this depended on the stabilisation of this specific form of government by military force (which had not been available to the king in the same degree). When this stabilising factor of the Protectorate disappeared with the death of Oliver Cromwell, as his son Richard (designated by his father as successor) had no political support in the army, the structural preconditions for the specific government form of the Protectorate also broke down. Likewise did those for the political dominance of the ‘new men' in local administration and in Parliament.
The different forms of government of the year 1659 - the dissolution of Par-liament on 22 April; the flight of Richard Cromwell; the re-introduction of the so-called Rump Parliament of 1648 and the end of the Protectorate on 7 May, the military dismissal of the Rump Parliament on 26 December - were all stages in this process of decay. Because the combination of political repression and political disillusion had in the meantime deprived the regiments of their political character, there was no longer any basis for army unity, no matter 
how tense. When the military commanders found themselves in different political camps in January 1660, they could use the soldiers under their com-mand for competing political aims. General Monk was the most successful. He led the divisions under his command into London and summoned once more the Long Parliament 'purged' in 1648. This made the restoration of the monarchy certain. In London, the bells of all churches rang out the same eve-ning, and the bonfires that were lit in the streets cast a glow over the city.246
On 8 May 1660, Charles II was proclaimed king of England by a newly elected Parliament.
c.2. Results of the Revolution
Certainly a revolution, but not the end of an era
In his well-known presentation of England's social and economic history from 1530 to 1780, Christopher Hill treats the decade of the 1640s as 'the end of medieval England'.247 Old institutions were abolished, above all the 'royal bureaucracy'.248 This supposedly introduced a change in economic structure. In place of an 'economic reform the basis of which were the monopoly companies', Hill saw the 'complete integration of English trade on the basis of a national monopoly, in which the state took on a leading role'.249 (The former monopoly companies were in no way completely abolished, but rather reached their apogee in the eighteenth century, especially the East India Company; but Hill leaves this out of the equation, as he does the continuance of tithes.) For Hill, the English Revolution really did do away with the 'feudal system'. Yet even so, this did not mean for him the immediate transition to capitalism. What arose instead was, rather, a kind of capitalism 'in spe’. For 'the political and legal conditions for further development had been created'.250”翻译为中文

以这种方式，军队形成了一种中度激进的政治立场（尽管它没有包含对教会的具体调整方案，甚至——如常被假设的——对完全良心自由的要求）。1648年所谓的第二次内战通过军事手段解决了权力争夺。其历史，正如温斯顿·丘吉尔恰当地写道，是“简短且简单”的。国王、贵族、平民、绅士和商人、城市和乡村、主教和长老会派、苏格兰军队、威尔士人民和海军，所有这些势力都对抗新模范军。而这支军队击败了他们联合的力量。

总结这些事件意味着引入许多不同的元素——反清教徒的民众起义和抵抗、针对县级委员会的请愿和大规模示威、伦敦商人对与国王和解的压力、海军的兵变以及为国王而战的苏格兰军队的斗争，以及议会力量在全国分发的公开辩护。所有这些我们必须在此略过。

在击败保皇党势力和所有起来反对军队及县级委员会的人之后，议会于1648年8月开始与国王进行和平谈判。议会要求对民兵的指挥权为期二十年，议会参与国王顾问的选择和长老会教会秩序。议会多数派——包括许多此前可以算作独立派的成员——准备接受国王最终同意的少数让步，尽管军队提出了强烈抗议和主要书面投诉，但以此为基础与君主和解。这已经表明，现在的争论远不止于君主权力的范围。出于与1660年更成功尝试相同的原因，议会多数派于1648年12月4日投票恢复君主制 —— “早了十一年”。

两天后，当军队控制了民事权力时，这一发展被阻止。议会虽然继续存在，但在政治上被“清洗”了支持与国王和解的成员。“普赖德的清洗”，即由普赖德上校将一些议员监禁的行为，使得谴责和处决国王成为可能。这是议会中温和势力和大人物向军队中的激进民众运动的最后让步。

在1649年1月国王被处决后，英格兰成为了共和国（尽管即使在那时，许多议员宁愿任命新国王也不愿废除君主制）。上议院被废除，紧随其后的是1650年9月执行的自伊丽莎白时代宗教定居以来强制周日参加教堂的刑法。但这几乎完成了1648-9年革命的制度性成果。对平等派要求民主化贵族统治的提法再也没有提及。相反，平等派的激进领袖被监禁，1649年5月激进团队的叛乱被暴力镇压。军队因此再次成为政治的工具。

然而，这种工具现在要捍卫的特定形式的贵族统治尚未确定。随后的十一年，直到君主制正式恢复——实际上，在护国主制下已经重新引入了相当程度的中央集权的个人占有——因此以争夺政治最高权或更准确地说，统治阶层内部权力持有结构的争斗为特征。

继续存在着废除贵族特权的要求。但随着1649年叛乱的失败，实现社会革命或共和要求的可能性——无论多么微弱——最终被排除。从现在开始，所有寻求深远变革的人再次被迫回到按照传统做法被视为“叛乱”的论点和表达形式。宗教激进分子，特别是“第五君权”的倡导者（即将赎回之前四个罪恶统治时期的耶稣基督的王权），仍然可以有一定的影响。因为，在17世纪，宗教论点的可能范围仍然远大于政治和社会批评的论点。但即使对地球上天国的到来的希望，也无法长期抗衡更直接的统治利益。对那些“真正”的平等派（更著名的称为挖掘者）在1649年后在一些小公社中开始实践的土地公有形式也是如此。到了1651年，这些实验被强制结束。

共和国、社会革命和千年王国派的民众运动在其目标上相互竞争，并未为革命的持续提供任何基础。然而，它们确实对现有权力持有者构成了挑衅，在这个程度上，它们也影响了权力分配的冲突。尽管如此，后来被称为间断时期的那些年的政治斗争本质上是“绅士”之间的事务。这些政治格局发生了变化：特别是县级行政和军队指挥官之间的“新势力”。这些军队指挥官的政治统治在1655年引入担任地方州长的“少校”时达到顶峰，他们负有军事和文职双重责任（类似于法国的总督）。这种政治权力的最大程度集中——因为少校是中央政府的执行者，该政府受到军队控制（如果不是完全支配）——意味着对英格兰贵族普遍贵族统治的更深远攻击，比县级委员会已经制度化的还要深远。阻碍和公开抵抗很快破坏了这种集中权力的基础。之所以会发生这种情况，是因为政府在基于宗教和贸易的外交政策上，依赖于统治阶层的支持——这是通过武装力量来执行的。少校的废除标志了1657年奥利弗·克伦威尔尝试建立新的政治联盟。“守护者”赋予他的个人统治建立在他对军事力量的掌控上。然而，其实践——就像国王所拥有的个人权力一样——依赖于大部分统治阶层成员的合作。克伦威尔确实在这些人中找到了支持他政治策略的一群人：历史学家如今称之为“新克伦威尔派”。1657年请克伦威尔加冕为国王的请求说明了这一点；尽管克伦威尔的拒绝表明他很清楚，接受将使他失去军队的支持，从而失去他统治的不可或缺的基础。

克伦威尔作为守护者所拥有的个人统治一方面在制度上比被处决的国王减少，而另一方面，常备军队的存在极大地扩大了它。然而，这种统治的实践要求“绅士”的合作——否则，新的职务结构的资金来源和权威都无法实现。如果说守护者政府能够维持下来，即使许多领先的贵族家族拒绝合作或被排除在外，这取决于军事力量的稳定（这是国王无法以同等程度获得的）。当这一稳定因素随着奥利弗·克伦威尔的死亡消失，因为他的儿子理查德（被他的父亲指定为继承人）在军队中没有政治支持时，“守护者”政府特殊形式的结构性前提条件也崩溃了。同样，地方行政和议会中“新人”的政治主导地位也崩溃了。

1659年的不同政府形式——4月22日议会解散；理查德·克伦威尔的逃亡；1648年所谓的“残余议会”和守护者政府于5月7日结束；12月26日残余议会的军事解散——都是这一衰败过程的阶段。由于政治压迫和政治幻灭的结合使得军团失去了它们的政治特性，尽管多么紧张，军队的统一基础不再存在。当军事指挥官在1660年1月发现自己处于不同的政治阵营时，他们可以将手下的士兵用于竞争的政治目标。蒙克将军最为成功。他将其指挥的部队带入伦敦，并再次召集了1648年被“清洗”的长期议会。这确保了君主制的恢复。在伦敦，所有教堂的钟声在同一晚上齐鸣，街道上点燃的篝火在城市上空投下光芒。

1660年5月8日，查理二世被新选的议会宣布为英格兰国王。

c.2. 革命的结果
的确是一场革命，但不是一个时代的结束

在克里斯托弗·希尔著名的论述中，他处理了从1530年到1780年英格兰的社会和经济历史，将1640年代视为“中世纪英格兰的终结”。旧制度被废除，尤其是“皇家官僚体系”。据称，这导致了经济结构的变化。希尔认为，取代了基于垄断公司基础的“经济改革”的是“基于国家垄断的英格兰贸易的完全整合，国家在其中扮演了领导角色”。（以前的垄断公司并未完全被废除，而是在18世纪达到了顶峰，特别是东印度公司；但希尔没有考虑这一点，也没有考虑什一税的持续。）对于希尔来说，英格兰革命确实废除了“封建制度”。然而，即使如此，这对他来说并不意味着直接过渡到资本主义。取而代之的是，相当于一种资本主义的“即将到来”。因为“进一步发展的政治和法律条件已经建立”。

请将“Hill's most important evidence for this thesis is the Navigation Acts. It is incontestable that these promoted a change in the economic system of the ancien régime, yet the causal connection that Hill takes for granted between these new regulations and the struggles to possess the means of rule is indeed open to challenge. The Navigation Acts can much more readily be interpreted as a result of the Peace of Westphalia. For English merchants who had profited throughout the wars of religion on the Continent by the neutrality of their own dynasty found themselves exposed after 1648 to a ruinous Dutch competition. It can hardly be convincingly argued that no measures could have been taken against this state of affairs without the Revolution, as many decisions had already been taken in support of trading interests. It must be granted to Hill that the application of the Navigation Acts, in so far as this was successful (one should not have too exaggerated an idea of this) would not have been possible without the navy, and this was undoubtedly a product of Parliament's struggle with the king. For the navy, when it was properly used militarily in the second half of the 1640s, and expanded further as a result, served in particular to prevent the intervention of foreign powers in support of Charles I and subsequently his son, who was already recognised by the Scots as Charles II. Hans-Christoph Junge has shown251 how London merchants of this time took a pressing interest in the expansion of the navy, especially because they were able to make immediate use of it. The merchants of the Colonial Interloping Trade, however, only used the opportunities it presented to obtain advantages vis-à-vis earlier leading trading circles in the City. Such competitive struggles between different trading interests cannot be immediately interpreted as a victory of economic ‘progressives' against representatives of ‘outdated' eco-nomic structures. It should finally be borne in mind that the ‘national trading interest' emphasised by Hill was quite evidently not strong enough to keep the navy to the size it had reached in the civil war (i.e. for purposes that were not immediately those of trade policy). Already during the Interregnum, part of the navy was decommissioned for fiscal reasons.252 If, after the Restoration, the fleet was not further reduced, unlike the army, this is far more readily attributable to the naval mutiny against the rule of the army command (hence in favour of the monarchy) than to any ‘national trading interest'. Towards the end of the 1660s, moreover, the crown had to abandon to a large extent the preservation of the fleet, as the plague and the Great Fire of London made its financing and manning difficult. The profits expected from the deployment of naval forces in battle, moreover, failed to materialise.253 Overly one-sided economic interpretations of the English Revolution are also faulted by the fact that Cromwell was ready to make a rapid peace with Holland founded on religious policy, despite conflicting mercantile interests. The so-called Grand Design, the quest for colonial conquests, served fiscal purposes above all. It, too, cannot simply be attributed to the victory of new social forces.
There was in England of the 1640s simply no politically decisive supporting group for the economic interest that was in the capitalist sense progressive, perhaps even 'national', and that the Revolution allegedly helped to prevail. In this connection, it is impossible to take seriously the claim that the twentyeight economic policy demands that the City of London presented to Parliament in 1640 were substantially different from those formulated in 1637.254 In the meantime, however, struggles had taken place that fundamentally altered the composition of the corporation and secured the support of London for the parliamentary cause. The 'new men' on the Common Council did indeed criticise the special privileges of the monopoly companies, but they did not demand their complete dissolution, let alone freedom for all trading and commercial activity.255 Just like their predecessors, they wished to maintain the traditional rights and privileges of the aldermen, vis-à-vis not just the government, but also the City population.
If our analysis here is less impressed by the changes in the organisational forms of trade that were demanded and realised, there is one key structural precondition (among others still to be considered) for the early breakthrough of capitalism in England that has special importance: the fact that most peasants here could not assert their property in the land they tilled with the sanction of generalised force. During the English Revolution, feudal tenures were abolished in 1646 along with the court of Warde, i.e. those fiscal burdens that had been imposed on the property of the lords for the benefit of the crown. A corresponding transformation of copyhold tenures, however, was not accom-plished. This form of limited possession of the means of production was thus not changed in the course of the Revolution. But whilst the abolition of feudal tenures held by vassals of the crown was only of secondary importance for the capitalisation of the rural economy, since it now represented no more than a specific form of taxation on hereditary landed property, the legal form of copyhold immensely facilitated the transition to capitalist farming. This means however that preconditions for the capitalisation of agricultural production came into being long before the revolutionary conflicts of the midseventeenth century. Emphasis on this point does not mean denying those structural effects of the Revolution that we shall deal with below. But their relevance to the development of capitalist forms of production and appropriation has to be assessed as rather small, if they are judged on the basis of a theory of economic transformation in which the social form of production is central, and not just the development of trade. The revolutionary events of 1640-60 had their effects on the social forms of production and appropriation. But these effects were not as simple as is suggested by lists of legal regulations, reference to trade wars and the development of postal communication. All these are changes in the structural matrix of the form of commodity production that had long been politically sanctioned in England. The extent of this political sanctioning contracted in the wake of the political conflicts. But there was no supporting group for its abolition, as we have already shown.
The same goes also for the ‘destruction of the royal bureaucracy' that Hill emphasises. For whilst Parliament and later the army leadership did indeed find themselves forced to invent new forms of government and thus also replace a part (!) of their former personnel, there still lacked any supporting group for a permanent depersonalisation of power of office that would have a more long-term effect. We shall examine below the successful repulsion of the ‘new men' by the former influential families. The first point to establish here is the failure of legal reform.
There were certainly a notable number of pamphlets that called for a com-prehensive legal reform, and Cromwell himself held the
law as it is now constituted, serves only to maintain the lawyers and to encourage the rich to oppress the poor.256
Yet these demands had little effect. The seventeenth century saw a series of notable improvements, especially in penal law, which were not interrupted by the Restoration. But there was no political basis for a reform that would meet the interests of office-holders - from prison governors through Justices of the Peace to the judges in the royal court of Chancery.257 The actual reform was limited to improvements in the legal practice of the ancien régime, i.e. a correc-tive to personal rule in the realm of jurisprudence, but not its abolition.
The smooth course which the Restoration took in the realm of ‘intellectual life' also shows how there was no supporting group in place even for a schol-arly critique of the ancien regime's forms of rule. If Charles I really had ‘alien-ated' scholars,258 the great majority of them were readily integrated into these forms of rule by his son.
Against the assumption that the English Revolution brought about a new era - one that was ‘bourgeois' in tendency, even if the actual bourgeoisie were missing - we shall start by stressing these continuities. This does not mean an attempt to prioritise the longue durée as against the ‘history of events', nor the rejection on principle of ‘political' historiography; it is simply an attempt to give these greater precision in connection with a structural analysis of the forms of rule of the English ancien régime. In connection with this presentation of the successful maintenance of a long-established possession of rule that had been temporarily shaken by the Revolution, these changes in the ruling structures of the ancien régime will now be sketched out. According to the thesis to be developed here, these are partly connected with the revolutionary events of the mid and late seventeenth century. At the same time, this partial transformation of the ancien régime is attributable to those social changes that led to a new internal structure of the English ruling estates.
Reconstitution of estate possession of locally generalised rule
The form of government of the Protectorate had its most important social basis in those circles that were called at the time the ‘new men'. They sat on the County Committees, they became major-generals, Members of Parliament, officers and judges. The fact that their interest in maintaining the changed situation, under conditions of persistent social conflict, high taxation and the military engagement in Ireland, Scotland and at sea, was not sufficient to stabilise this form of government, is already made clear in the way that military rule over local administration was again relaxed, a kind of upper house introduced, and the attempt made to resolve the government's financial problems by the risky venture of colonial conquest. For without a reduction in the tax burden, there could not be any long-run toleration of the new forces on the part of the formerly ruling estates, let alone support.
The Restoration ended the rule of the ‘new men' and to this extent meant the reintroduction of previous conditions. Local administration became once again the generalised possession of local leading families. Justices of the Peace appointed from their milieu functioned as their representatives. The conclusion that Philip Jenkins reached for Glamorgan in Wales, that ‘the justices were simply the official manifestation of the gentry class',259 can be generalised to England as a whole.”翻译为中文

希尔对这一论题的最重要证据是《航海法案》。这些法案无可争议地促进了旧制度经济体系的变革，然而，希尔认为理所当然的这些新规定与争夺统治手段的斗争之间的因果联系的确是值得质疑的。《航海法案》更容易被解释为《威斯特伐利亚和约》的结果。因为在大陆的宗教战争期间，英国商人通过本国王朝的中立获利，但在1648年之后发现自己暴露在灾难性的荷兰竞争之下。很难令人信服地论证，如果没有革命，就无法对这种情况采取措施，因为许多支持贸易利益的决策已经被做出。必须承认希尔的观点，即《航海法案》的应用在成功的情况下（不应对此抱有太高的期望）离不开海军，而海军无疑是议会与国王斗争的产物。因为在1640年代后半期正确地军事使用并因此进一步扩张的海军，特别是为了阻止外国势力支持查理一世及其后被苏格兰承认为查理二世的儿子进行干预。汉斯-克里斯托夫·容格已经展示了当时的伦敦商人对扩张海军的迫切兴趣，特别是因为他们能够立即利用它。然而，殖民地非法贸易的商人只是利用它提供的机会，以获得相对于城市内早期领先贸易圈的优势。不同贸易利益之间的竞争斗争不能立即被解释为经济“进步分子”对“过时”经济结构代表的胜利。最后应该记住的是，希尔强调的“国家贸易利益”显然不足以保持海军在内战中达到的规模（即非贸易政策的目的）。在间歇期间，由于财政原因，部分海军被解散。如果在复辟后，舰队未像军队那样进一步缩减，这更多地归因于海军对军队指挥官统治的叛变（因此支持君主制）而非任何“国家贸易利益”。到了1660年代末，此外，由于瘟疫和伦敦大火使得舰队的资金和人员难以维持，王室不得不在很大程度上放弃保持舰队。此外，从海战中部署海军力量所期望的利润也未实现。对英格兰革命的过于片面的经济解释也受到质疑，因为尽管存在商业利益冲突，克伦威尔准备与荷兰迅速和平，这是基于宗教政策。所谓的“大设计”——对殖民地征服的追求——首先是出于财政目的。它同样不能简单归因于新的社会力量的胜利。

1640年代的英格兰根本就没有政治上决定性的支持团体，支持那些在资本主义意义上是进步的、甚至可能是“民族”的经济利益，这是革命据称帮助实现的。在这方面，声称伦敦市在1640年向议会提出的二十八项经济政策要求与1637年提出的要求实质不同的说法是无法被认真对待的。与此同时，进行了斗争，从根本上改变了公司的组成，确保了伦敦对议会事业的支持。在市议会的“新人”确实批评了垄断公司的特权，但他们没有要求彻底解散它们，更不用说为所有贸易和商业活动自由了。就像他们的前任一样，他们希望维护市政府成员面对政府乃至城市居民的传统权利和特权。

如果我们对要求和实现的贸易组织形式的变化印象不深，那么英格兰早期资本主义突破的一个关键结构性前提（还有其他待考量的因素）具有特殊重要性：大多数农民无法以普遍力量的支持来维护他们耕种的土地产权。在英格兰革命期间，1646年随着守卫法庭的废除，封建地契被废除，即对领主产权征收的那些财政负担为了国王的利益而设。然而，相应地改变佃农契约并未完成。因此，在革命过程中，这种有限的生产资料占有形式并未改变。但是，封建地契的废除对农村经济资本化只是次要的重要性，因为它现在不过是对世袭土地产权征税的一种特定形式，佃农契约的法律形式极大地促进了向资本主义农业的过渡。这意味着，农业生产资本化的前提条件早在17世纪中叶革命冲突之前就已经形成。强调这一点并不意味着否认我们将在下面讨论的革命的结构效应。但如果根据以社会生产形式为中心、而不仅仅是贸易发展的经济转型理论来判断，它们对资本主义生产和占有形式发展的相关性必须被视为相当小。1640-60年的革命事件对生产和占有的社会形式确实产生了影响。但这些影响并不像法律法规清单、贸易战和邮政通信发展那样简单。所有这些都是在英格兰长期以来在政治上得到认可的商品生产形式的结构矩阵中的变化。由于政治冲突，这种政治认可的程度缩小了。但正如我们已经展示的那样，并没有一个支持团体支持其废除。

同样，对于希尔强调的“皇家官僚机构的破坏”也是如此。尽管议会和后来的军队领导确实发现自己被迫发明新的政府形式，从而也替换了部分（！）以前的人员，但仍然缺乏任何支持团体支持一个对公职权力产生更长期影响的持久去人格化。我们将在下面检查“新人”被以前有影响力的家族成功击退的情况。首先要确定的是法律改革的失败。

确实有大量的小册子呼吁进行全面的法律改革，克伦威尔本人认为
现行法律只是为了维护律师的利益，鼓励富人压迫穷人。

然而这些要求收效甚微。17世纪见证了一系列尤其是在刑法方面的显著改善，这些改善并未因复辟而中断。但这并没有为满足公职持有者的利益提供政治基础——从监狱长到治安法官再到皇家大法院的法官。实际的改革仅限于对旧制度法律实践的改进，即对司法领域个人统治的纠正，而非其废除。

复辟在“知识生活”领域的顺利进行也表明，即使是对旧制度统治形式的学术批评也没有支持团体。如果查理一世真的“疏远”了学者，他的儿子很容易将他们大多数重新融入这些统治形式。

反对假设英格兰革命带来了一个新时代的假设——这个时代倾向于“资产阶级”，即使实际的资产阶级缺席——我们将首先强调这些连续性。这并不意味着试图将长期持续性优先于“事件历史”，也不是原则上拒绝“政治”历史学；这只是试图在与英格兰旧制度的统治形式的结构分析结合时给予这些更多的精确性。在关于革命暂时动摇了由来已久的统治占有得以成功维护的呈现中，现在将概述旧制度的统治结构的这些变化。根据我们将发展的论点，这些部分与17世纪中晚期的革命事件有关。同时，这种旧制度的部分转变归因于导致英格兰统治阶层内部结构新变化的社会变革。

重建地方普遍贵族统治的贵族占有
护国主制的政府形式在当时被称为“新人”的圈子中有其最重要的社会基础。他们在县级委员会中占据席位，成为少校、议会成员、军官和法官。这些人对维持变革后的局势的兴趣，在持续的社会冲突、高税负和在爱尔兰、苏格兰和海上的军事行动条件下，不足以稳定这种政府形式，这一点从军队对地方行政的统治再次放松、引入一种类似上议院的机构并试图通过殖民征服的冒险来解决政府财政问题的方式中已经很清楚了。因为如果不减少税收负担，就不可能让原本占统治地位的阶层长期容忍新势力，更不用说支持了。

复辟结束了“新人”的统治，从这个意义上说，意味着恢复了以前的条件。地方行政再次成为地方领先家族的普遍占有。从他们的圈子中任命的治安法官充当了他们的代表。菲利普·詹金斯对威尔士格拉摩根得出的结论，“法官只是绅士阶层的官方表现”，可以推广到整个英格兰。

请将“With the disappearance of the ‘new men' from the key positions of power that had in part been newly created by the Revolution, there emerged a defence of local possession of rule against claims of decision by the central government. It is true that the Revolution had only been possible because in England there had long been a generalised possession of rule, not merely formally but in actual fact. For it was only on this basis that interests, demands and political goals could be experienced and conceived as general ones. The course of the Revolution even strengthened such generalisation in the long term. For in the wake of its conflicts, not only did Puritanism develop into a view of life that laid claim to order the whole kingdom,260 there also arose the first regular political factions. All the same, the Restoration saw the victory of a strongly anti-centralist tendency, and the dominant attitude of loyalists towards the crown, one that marked the first ten years of Charles Il's reign, did not prevent its interventions into local affairs being tolerated only to a limited extent. Since the abolition of the Star Chamber, the government lacked even this instance for controlling the Justices of the Peace. From the 1670s, and especially in the reign of James II, these often practised systematic obstruction.261 Support for the royal house was clearly limited by localism. This already gives an indication of how localism under the Restoration, like so many other things, cannot just be seen as the continuation of a tradition that had been briefly interrupted. There had certainly been earlier interests that were partially oriented to general structures, and the very existence of a Parliament that held sway over the whole kingdom (and not just over particular local estates) inflected political strategies. Yet the localism of the post-Revolutionary era had its base less in the social and cultural particularities of individual counties, than in a political strategy of anti-centralism that was widespread among the ruling estates. Autonomy became a more significant issue than it had been before the Revolution.
One of the earliest expressions of this new situation was the conflict over the militia. Charles II had been granted command of the militia, and in 1662 even the right to levy financial contributions for an expanded deployment of the militia in the next three years.262 The militia commanders were also appointed by the king, just as before the Revolution. He could, if he liked, not only personally nominate the lords lieutenant, but also their deputies (otherwise, these were appointed by the lord lieutenant in question). Yet even the Parliament of the 1660s, with its strongly monarchist orientation, rejected all attempts to make the militia a component of a standing army.263 At the same time, the ‘Cavalier Parliament' limited the actual power of the crown's military representatives in the counties, by reducing the resources available to the militia. (The regulations that had exempted the poorer rural population from militia service were now abolished.) Though the king's authority over the militia was very wide-ranging in formal terms, it was considerably reduced by fiscal measures.
More decisive in the course of time, however, were the political limitations of this aspect of rule. For the local gentry increasingly rejected the deployment of the militia for disciplining their fellows. Although the great majority of the English nobility supported the church-policy strategy of conformism, which Parliament had proposed to the crown, and though the experiences of the revolutionary years had aroused or fuelled a fear of radical (popular) religious movements, the members of the ruling estates maintained in practice a de facto toleration towards those of their own number with whom they were personally acquainted.
The question of the suppression of Catholicism reached a political climax in the so-called ‘exclusion crisis'. This was brought about by a number of Mem- bers of Parliament seeking to exclude James from succession to the throne, as he was known to be friendly to the Catholic cause. The crown then tried to use the militia as the instrument of a particular political orientation. The lords lieutenant, at least those that were not opposed to this,264 purged the militia of politically unreliable officers, and their deputies rejected recruits on political grounds. By this purging of the militia, Charles II destroyed its organisational basis. For the English militia could only operate as a military force that, while commanded by the king, was established in collaboration with the local ruling estates.265
Just as with the question of the king's command over the militia, so the reconstitution of estate power manifested itself as a limitation on the central government's freedom of action in its rule over the church. In 1661, the king had regained possession of this. The far-reaching extent of the Restoration is indeed made clear by the fact that Charles II was able to disappoint the expectations of many Presbyterians266 that their consent to his ascending the throne would be repaid in his religious policy. The rule of the bishops was re-imposed, not only as an important field of royal patronage, but at the same time a cornerstone of royalist policy in the House of Lords.
Charles II was unyielding on several key decisions of religious policy, espe-cially the question of the succession to the throne of his Catholic brother, and he was in a position at this time to impose his will.267 On the other hand, he bowed to the resistance of Parliament to an official policy of religious tolerance. In 1662 the crown withdrew a legislative proposal on this question, and in 1663 the king was even prepared to accept the formal exclusion of non-Anglican subjects from all public offices. (Following the Test Act passed at this time, every official now had to swear an oath to follow the Anglican faith.) The estate demand for conformity coincided fully with the claim of local noble families to decide for themselves on the use of force against Nonconformist neighbours. For religious conformism was seen by the politically ruling groups in the decades following the Restoration as the most important guarantee of desirable social and political behaviour. This was the very reason that the first major opposition to the restored monarchy was aroused
by the possible succession of a Catholic to the throne. If in their immediate local milieu a certain (political) attitude was already known and familiar, the local ruling families could refrain from enforcing religious conformity in the individual case. The oath continued in the seventeenth century to function as a binding means of rule. Yet, after the Revolution, private belief was scarcely prosecuted any more. It was even tolerated at first that many officials only met the provisions of the Test Act in so far as they received the sacraments of the Anglican rite on their appointment. Precisely because the Anglican faith functioned as a guarantee of social and political stability, public worship was above all a political token.268 Only in 1711 was the legal attempt made to forbid office-holders from a purely formal conformism.
Through their representatives in both Houses of Parliament, the ruling estates managed to put through a rigorous religious policy. The principal rea-son for the failure of James II was that he was in no way prepared to meet this requirement. Moreover, the most important basis for estate collaboration in the determination of religious practice by authority did not lie in Parliament. For the restoration of episcopacy, along with the possibility this gave of controlling the content of sermons and priests themselves, should not blind us to the fact that rule over religious practice, and thus the regulation of good order, had very largely passed with the Restoration into the generalised possession of the ruling estate. The higher church court remained abolished, and the lower church courts depended on the Justices of the Peace for the execution of the penalties they imposed. The result of this was that church jurisdiction gradually went into decline, so that public morality fell completely into the control of generalised estate rule. Under these conditions, conformity with the Anglican church meant first and foremost participation in a model of respectability that was sanctioned by authority. In this way, Church doctrine became virtually incorporated into the estate determination of 'good order'. Whereas episcopal rule and monarchical power had formed before the Revolution a far-reaching and effective unity, the church of the Restoration era was far more clearly integrated than before into the generalised estate power.
The introduction in 1662 of censorship of books that were judged ‘contrary to good life or good manners'269 corresponded to these developments at the legal level. For the revolutionary claim of religious radicalism to organise society in such a fashion that it met the wishes of God had already been challenged under the Interregnum and completely lost any social legitimacy with the Restoration. All that remained of the kingdom of God on earth was now ‘good custom' defined by the ruling estate - but sanctioned as before by religion.
The power to define the structures of ‘good order' shows the double orien-tation of estate rule under the Restoration. It was directed on one hand against comprehensive regulation claims of any central government, and despite its loyalty it still set limits to the restored personal rule of the king. On the other hand, it sought to maintain a dominant position against new social groups on the rise and against the common people.
The radical popular movements of the revolutionary era had certainly been defeated already under the Protectorate, and for this reason the militia was only rarely called upon to repress popular uprisings in the following decades. There were still Leveller talk and publications in the second half of the sev-enteenth century, but as a movement, the Levellers had long since failed. It was only the Monmouth rebellion of 1685 that brought a new revolt by the common people, with the militia initially having serious difficulty in mastering the armed peasants. On the whole, however, and despite the long-lasting fear of an uprising by discharged soldiers and officers, the restoration of previous structures of rule could to a large extent be accomplished and maintained without the use of military or criminal sanction. The widespread desire for peaceful conditions certainly made its contribution to this, and so did the repression already carried out under the Protectorate. To this extent, the ruling estates could simply resurrect their long established position of rule over the common people after the years of Revolution and the Protectorate, more or less unscathed.
The restoration of estate rule meant that the public outside the ruling estates were once more excluded from the processes of political decision. Not only were the old limitations on suffrage for the House of Commons re-established,270 but mass petitions to Parliament were forbidden, while any kind of express political utterance against the established authorities was defined as rebellion. This stabilisation of rule was formally sanctioned in 1661 with the passage of a law under which political criticism could be prosecuted as 'treason' against the kingdom. In this way, law was once again made into an instrument of estate rule with an authoritarian interpretation.”翻译为中文

随着“新人”从革命部分新创立的关键权力职位中消失，出现了针对中央政府决策权要求的地方统治占有权的捍卫。的确，革命之所以成为可能，是因为英格兰长期以来就存在了普遍的统治占有权，不仅仅是名义上的，而是实际上的。因为只有在这种基础上，利益、要求和政治目标才能被理解和构想为普遍性的。革命的过程甚至从长远来看加强了这种普遍性。因为在其冲突的余波中，清教主义不仅发展成为一种声称要整个王国的生活观，还出现了第一批规律的政治派别。尽管如此，复辟见证了强烈反中央主义倾向的胜利，而忠于皇室的态度在查尔斯二世统治的最初十年中虽未阻止其对地方事务的干预，但这种干预只能在有限范围内被容忍。自从星室法庭被废除以来，政府甚至缺乏这一实例来控制治安法官。从17世纪70年代起，特别是在詹姆士二世统治期间，这些往往采取系统性阻挠行为。对皇室的支持显然受到地方主义的限制。这已经显示出复辟后的地方主义，像许多其他事物一样，不仅仅是被短暂中断的传统的延续。当然曾经有部分以普遍结构为导向的利益存在，而整个王国（而不仅仅是特定地方贵族）支配下的议会的存在，影响了政治策略。然而，革命后时代的地方主义基础更少在于个别郡的社会和文化特性，而更多在于统治阶层普遍采用的反中央主义政治策略中。自治成为一个比革命前更重要的问题。

这一新情况的最早表现之一是关于民兵的冲突。查尔斯二世被授予民兵的指挥权，并在1662年甚至获得了未来三年扩大民兵部署的财政征集权。民兵指挥官也由国王任命，就像革命前一样。如果他愿意，他不仅可以亲自提名郡中尉，还可以提名他们的副手（否则，由相关的郡中尉任命）。然而，即便是具有强烈君主主义倾向的1660年代的议会也拒绝了所有试图将民兵作为常备军组成部分的尝试。与此同时，“骑士议会”限制了皇室军事代表在郡县中的实际权力，通过减少民兵可用的资源。（免除较贫困农村人口民兵服役的规定现在被废除。）尽管国王对民兵的权力在形式上非常广泛，但通过财政措施被大大削弱。

然而，随着时间的推移，更为决定性的是这一统治方面的政治限制。因为地方绅士越来越多地拒绝部署民兵来管束他们的同僚。尽管绝大多数英格兰贵族支持议会向皇室提议的一致宗教政策策略，尽管革命年代的经历激发或加剧了对激进（民众）宗教运动的恐惧，但统治阶层成员在实践中对他们亲自熟悉的同僚保持了事实上的宽容。

压制天主教的问题在所谓的“排除危机”中达到了政治高潮。这是由一些议会议员试图排除詹姆士继承王位引发的，因为他被知道是亲天主教的。然后，皇室试图将民兵用作特定政治倾向的工具。至少那些不反对此举的郡中尉，清除了民兵队伍中政治上不可靠的军官，他们的副手出于政治原因拒绝了新兵。通过清洗民兵，查尔斯二世摧毁了其组织基础。因为英格兰民兵只能作为一支军事力量运作，虽然由国王指挥，但与地方统治阶层合作建立。

与国王对民兵的指挥权问题一样，贵族权力的重建表现为对中央政府行动自由的限制，在其对教会的统治上也是如此。1661年，国王重新获得了这一权力。复辟的广泛程度确实表明，查尔斯二世能够令许多长老会派信徒失望，他们原以为对他登基的支持会在他的宗教政策中得到回报。重新实行了主教的统治，不仅作为皇室赞助的重要领域，同时也是皇室政策在上议院的基石。

查尔斯二世在几个关键的宗教政策决定上毫不妥协，特别是关于他的天主教兄弟继承王位的问题，他当时有能力强加自己的意愿。另一方面，他向议会对官方宗教容忍政策的抵制低头。1662年，皇室撤回了关于这个问题的立法提案，到了1663年，国王甚至准备接受非英国国教徒从所有公职中被正式排除。（根据当时通过的测试法案，每位官员现在必须宣誓信奉英国国教。）贵族对一致性的要求与地方贵族家族决定自行对待不顺从邻居使用武力的权利完全一致。因为在复辟后的几十年中，宗教一致性被政治统治集团视为社会和政治行为的最重要保障。这正是第一次对恢复的君主制的主要反对由天主教徒可能继承王位的可能性引发的原因。如果他们在直接的地方环境中已经熟知并熟悉某个（政治）态度，地方统治家族可以在个别案例中免于执行宗教一致性。誓言在17世纪仍然用作统治的约束手段。然而，革命后，私人信仰几乎不再受到起诉。起初甚至还容忍许多官员在任命时只是符合英国国教仪式接受圣餐的规定。正因为英国国教起到了社会和政治稳定的保障作用，公开崇拜首先是政治象征。直到1711年才试图法律禁止官员仅形式上的一致主义。

通过议会两院的代表，统治阶层设法通过了严格的宗教政策。詹姆士二世之所以失败的主要原因是，他根本没有准备满足这一要求。此外，贵族在决定宗教实践上的合作最重要的基础并不在议会。因为随着主教权力的恢复，这给控制讲道内容和牧师本身提供了可能性，但我们不应该忽视，在复辟后，宗教实践的统治，从而对良好秩序的规范，很大程度上已经过渡到统治贵族的普遍占有。高级教会法庭仍然被废除，而低级教会法庭对其判决的执行依赖于治安法官。结果，教会司法逐渐衰落，公共道德完全受到贵族统治的控制。在这些条件下，与英国国教的一致性首先意味着参与到由权威认可的尊重模式中。因此，教会教义实际上被融入到贵族对“良好秩序”的决定中。复辟时期的教会与革命前形成的广泛有效的主教统治和君权的统一相比，更清晰地融入到了贵族普遍权力中。

1662年针对被认为“违反良好生活或良好风俗”的书籍引入的审查制度在法律层面上与这些发展相一致。因为宗教激进主义对组织社会以满足上帝意愿的革命主张已经在内政期间受到挑战，并在复辟后彻底失去了社会合法性。剩下的上帝在地球上的王国现在只是由统治阶层定义的“良好习俗”——但仍然像以前一样由宗教认可。

对“良好秩序”结构的定义显示了复辟下贵族统治的双重取向。一方面，它针对任何中央政府的全面调控要求，并且尽管忠诚，仍然限制了恢复的君王个人统治的自由。另一方面，它试图对抗新兴社会群体和普通民众保持主导地位。

革命时期的激进民众运动在护国主时期就已经被击败，因此在接下来的几十年里，民兵很少被用来镇压民众起义。17世纪下半叶仍有平等派言论和出版物，但作为一股运动，平等派早已失败。只有1685年蒙茅斯叛乱引发了普通民众的新起义，民兵最初在控制武装农民方面遇到了严重困难。然而，总的来说，尽管长期以来一直担心被遣散士兵和军官起义，恢复以前的统治结构在很大程度上可以在不使用军事或刑事制裁的情况下完成和维持。对和平条件的普遍渴望当然做出了贡献，护国主时期的镇压也是如此。在这个意义上，统治阶层可以在革命和护国主时期之后，几乎毫发无损地重新建立起他们对普通民众的长久以来的统治地位。

贵族统治的恢复意味着统治阶层之外的公众再次被排除在政治决策过程之外。不仅恢复了下议院选举的旧限制，对议会的大规模请愿被禁止，任何形式的针对既定当局的明确政治表态都被定义为叛乱。这种统治的稳定在1661年通过一项法律正式得到认可，根据该法律，政治批评可以被起诉为对王国的“叛国”。通过这种方式，法律再次成为以权威解释方式用作贵族统治工具。
